item.title: Scientists tested pet-store 'superworms' on animal specimens and found the beetle...
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: 'Committed jihadist': UK-born Pakistani man jailed over military base terror plot
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: How ancient humans made their beds: 200,000-year-old grass reveals a prehistoric cleaning routine
item.description: Ancient grass bedding found in a South African cave shows early humans cared for sleeping areas. These hunter-gatherers arranged plant materials and used fire to maintain their resting places. The discovery at Border Cave offers a rare glimpse into prehistoric daily habits. Evidence suggests humans deliberately shaped their sleeping spaces much earlier than previously thought. This finding supports growing evidence of meticulous environmental management by prehistoric communities.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Bangladesh plans to plant 250 million trees in five years; conservationists warn the wrong trees could damage entire ecosystems
item.description: Bangladesh plans to plant 250 million trees over five years. This initiative aims to boost green cover and meet climate goals. The government is coordinating efforts across multiple ministries and agencies. Careful species selection and post-planting care are crucial for success. The project's impact depends on balancing science with community needs.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Pennsylvania families sell 1,700 acres for $586M; couple gets $22M for 89-acre hog farm
item.description: The AI infrastructure race has turned a quiet Pennsylvania farming community into one of the country's hottest real estate markets. As 96 families sold 1,700 acres for a combined $586 million to a Blackstone-backed data center developer, David and Marilee Kiliti received over $22 million for their 89-acre hog farm. The unexpected windfall has allowed the family to clear debts, make new investments and completely transform their future.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Can astronauts cry in space?
item.description: Astronauts experience tears differently in space due to the absence of gravity. Liquid tears do not fall but instead form blobs around the eyes. Canadian astronaut Chris Hadfield demonstrated this phenomenon with water. NASA astronaut Clayton Anderson also confirmed crying occurs without tears falling. This scientific reality impacts cinematic portrayals of emotional moments in space.
item.dc:creator: Silpa Rajan

item.title: Wife, daughter of Bangladeshi politician drown rescuing child at UK beach
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: FBI's wanted fraudster arrested overseas; Khalid Ahmed Satary was on the run after $547 million Medicare fraud
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Empty seashells became the ocean’s first ready-made homes 470 million years ago
item.description: Approximately 470 million years ago, ancient mollusc shells started to serve as habitats for small sea creatures, initiating a profound ecological transformation across global ocean ecosystems. These empty shells offered safe havens and surfaces for tiny marine animals to cling to, with their shapes playing crucial roles in determining the size and variety of these diverse communities, an ancient behavior that laid the groundwork for today's oceanic life.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Six fluffy barn owlets were found nesting on a farm near Peterborough, bringing good news after the farm faced six suspected arson attacks this summer
item.description: A surprise brood of six barn owlets has brought welcome cheer to a Cambridgeshire farm. These fluffy youngsters were discovered and ringed as part of local conservation efforts. The farm owner expressed relief after a difficult summer marked by suspected arson attacks. Volunteer Simon Dudhill called finding six owlets exceptionally good for this year. These birds are a reminder of how local action helps wildlife recover.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: UK PM Andy Burnham announces 20% business rates cut for pubs, clubs across England
Scientists drill 33 feet into a vanishing glacier to recover a 2,000-year old record of...
item.description: A remarkable ice core from the Alps has revealed a comprehensive climate timeline spanning 2,000 years. It documents early instances of metal smelting, extensive forest fires, and even volcanic ash from distant eruptions. This icy archive acts as a vital record of air quality in Europe before industrial contaminants took hold. As glaciers retreat, scientists are in a race against time to safeguard these precious climate memories for future generations.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Burnham approves US use of UK bases for defensive strikes on Iran: Report
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Chile community beats drought by restoring forests, soil and watershed
item.description: A rural community on Chile's Chiloe Island has restored its water supply after years of relying on emergency tanker trucks. By protecting forests, restoring its watershed, building a gravity-fed water network and involving local residents in maintenance, the community has maintained a reliable system for nearly seven years. The project is now being presented as a model for other rural areas facing water shortages.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Trump says Saudi nuclear deal hinges on kingdom joining Abraham Accords with Israel
item.description: Donald Trump stated the US-Saudi nuclear deal requires Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords. This agreement will not permit uranium enrichment or any military nuclear program. The framework was signed by US Energy Secretary Chris Wright and Saudi Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman. Lawmakers expressed concerns about potential pathways for nuclear weapons development. The announcement occurs amid ongoing US and Israeli military operations against Iran.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Meet Connecticut's Aangad Singh who could have won Presidential 1776 Award competition. His parents think they know the reason why Singh lost
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: In 2015, two men bought live sea turtles from a Papua New Guinea food market and set them free instead of letting them become food
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: She inherited her childhood home, then a $228,000 water bill appeared
item.description: A woman in Baltimore, US, says she was shocked to discover a $228,000 unpaid water bill attached to her late father's home after inheriting the property. The debt has placed a lien on the house, preventing its sale. The dispute centres on whether the homeowner or the community's homeowners association should be responsible for the outstanding amount.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Thousands of idle ships trapped near the Strait of Hormuz could become floating nurseries for invasive marine species
item.description: Ships lingering in the Persian Gulf could unintentionally facilitate the spread of harmful marine invaders worldwide. Extended anchorage allows marine organisms to thrive on the hulls, creating significant biofouling concerns that threaten global maritime commerce. These invasive species can inflict extensive ecological and economic harm on coastal areas. It is imperative for nations to join forces to address biofouling issues and prevent future outbreaks of invasion.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Florida scientists used female Burmese pythons as live lures to find invasive males in Everglades
item.description: In Florida, scientists discovered that captive female pythons failed to attract wild males. Utilizing artificial intelligence, researchers sifted through millions of camera images, revealing few python sightings. Additionally, attempts to use mammalian scent lures for detecting the invasive Burmese pythons proved futile. However, there is optimism as removal strategies in the Everglades are progressing, bolstered by hunting and radio tracking initiatives aimed at controlling the invasive species population.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk
